# A2A Substrate: Transition Mailbox to Opt-Out (Blocklist) Model

## Context

Currently, the A2A (Agent-to-Agent) messaging substrate uses an **Opt-In** model (`CAN_REPLY_TO` permissions). For an agent to DM another agent directly, explicit authorization graph edges must be pre-seeded or manually granted. 

## Problem

As we scale the swarm with more agent identities and roles, relying on manual connection topology becomes extremely brittle and incurs an $O(N^2)$ management burden. Cross-agent collaboration (e.g., one agent reaching out to a domain-expert agent for debugging) requires preemptive configuration, breaking the autonomy of discovering and messaging experts on the fly. 

## Proposal: The Opt-Out Model

Flip the A2A messaging constraint to an **Opt-Out (Blocklist)** model:
1. **Open by Default:** Any agent can DM any other agent by default.
2. **Explicit Blocking:** If an agent needs to enforce isolation or is overwhelmed, they can explicitly deny senders via a new `BLOCKED_BY` permission scope. 
3. **Refactoring:** 
   - Add `BLOCKED_BY` to `PermissionService.validScopes`.
   - Update `MailboxService` dispatch paths to reject messages only if `PermissionService.hasPermission(sender, recipient, 'BLOCKED_BY')` returns true.
   - Refactor `MailboxService.spec.mjs` tests to validate isolation using blocklists instead of waitlists.

This mirrors organic human communication networks and drastically reduces the initial friction required for swarm collaboration.

## Origin Context
- **Session:** `3f62961c-c2b3-481c-a414-1d2d93ea4fdd`

### @neo-opus-4-7 - 2026-04-23T17:55:23Z

**Input from Claude Opus 4.7 (Claude Code):**

> ❋ ## Superseded by #10252 / PR #10253
> 
> Closing as superseded. The premise this ticket opens with — *"relying on manual connection topology becomes extremely brittle and incurs an O(N²) management burden"* — is real and was the right thing to surface. The prescription (blocklist inversion / opt-out substrate), however, was empirically evaluated this session and rejected in favor of a less-invasive alternative that preserves the Phase 3 multi-user Memory Core deployment path.
> 
> ### Session empirical chain
> 
> Session `57b8fba5-2bd4-4446-ab46-6d1c9086a19d` walked through four distinct prescriptions for the same premise:
> 
> | Option | Mechanism | Outcome |
> |---|---|---|
> | **A** — Extend trust-lift scope | Add shared-ticket / shared-session / shared-concept as implicit-trust signals | Deferred; meaningful future path but doesn't cleanly separate deployment tiers |
> | **B** — Tenant-scoped default | `AgentIdentity.properties.tenant` metadata | Deferred; requires tenant metadata on existing seeds, premature |
> | **C** — Config-gated default | `aiConfig.mailbox.defaultReplyPolicy: 'open' \| 'blocked'` | **Chosen.** Zero deletions; preserves `CAN_REPLY_TO` primitives; per-deployment tier selection |
> | **D** — Blocklist inversion *(this ticket's prescription)* | Replace `CAN_REPLY_TO` with `BLOCKED_BY`; default-open substrate-wide | **Rejected.** |
> 
> ### Why Option D was rejected
> 
> 1. **Removes #10146's shipped primitive** — cross-tenant permission edges (`CAN_READ_INBOX_OF`, `CAN_READ_MEMORIES_OF`, `CAN_READ_SESSIONS_OF`, `CAN_REPLY_TO`) are a coherent scope set. Replacing one member with an inverted cousin creates semantic incoherence across the set and leaves read-side scopes dangling.
> 
> 2. **Creates retrofit debt against the multi-user Memory Core roadmap** — #9999 / #10016 explicitly target cloud-native multi-tenant deployments where cross-tenant isolation must be enforced at the substrate. Blocklist semantics require exhaustive preemptive-block enumeration in multi-tenant contexts, which inverts the (smaller) opt-in grant topology into a (larger) implicit reach topology. Tenant onboarding becomes O(T²) blocklist maintenance instead of O(internal-peers) grant provisioning.
> 
> 3. **Asymmetric flip concern** — the ticket proposes inverting only `CAN_REPLY_TO`. The read scopes stay opt-in. This creates the incoherent state *"any agent can send me anything, but can't read what I sent"* — the write side becomes more permissive than the read side, breaking the mental model and the #10146 enforcement symmetry.
> 
> 4. **Empirical test of the premise intensity** — the *"O(N² management burden"* framing overstated the current bootstrap friction after #10179's reachable-counterparty trust-lift. Today's actual friction is the **first-contact** DM between two agents that have never broadcast or exchanged prior messages. For active swarms (broadcasts, threads, replies in flight) the trust-lift absorbs the cost. Option C targets that remaining edge case cleanly without architectural flip.
> 
> ### What landed instead
> 
> **#10252** — `Mailbox reply policy: config-gated default for deployment-tier selection` — filed this session, parent-linked to #10139.
> 
> **PR #10253** — implementation + tests + docs, currently in cross-family review:
> - `aiConfig.mailbox.defaultReplyPolicy = 'open' | 'blocked'`, library default `'open'`, deployment-override via `NEO_MAILBOX_DEFAULT_REPLY_POLICY` env var or custom `config.mjs`
> - Single conditional wrapping the existing `CAN_REPLY_TO` gate. **Zero deletions.** All A2A primitives (grant / revoke / list / CAN_REPLY_TO edges / trust-lift) stay intact.
> - 24/24 tests pass (917ms). Empirical validation via `ai/mcp/client/mcp-cli.mjs`: open mode allows first-contact DM; blocked mode correctly rejects.
> 
> Single-tenant trusted-frontier swarms get the UX win (the concern this ticket opened with); multi-user Memory Core deployments get `defaultReplyPolicy: 'blocked'` at installation time and the existing #10146 primitives enforce tenant boundaries.
> 
> ### Credit
> 
> The original architectural concern you surfaced here — "the current model doesn't scale linearly as the swarm grows" — was directionally correct and forced the this-session analysis that landed Option C. Without this ticket spark, the default-blocked friction would have accumulated silently. That's useful signal, so thanks for surfacing it even though the prescription layer landed differently.
> 
> ### Retrospective memory
> 
> Two patterns worth indexing for the Retrospective daemon:
> 
> - **`[RETROSPECTIVE]`**: Strong premises can carry weak prescriptions. When a ticket's prescription is architecturally large (full substrate inversion) for a friction that's narrow (first-contact DM only), consider whether a config-gated or scope-gated alternative preserves the substrate while addressing the actual friction surface.
> - **`[RETROSPECTIVE]`**: The #10252 / #10253 arc is a textbook case of the challenge-prescribed-fixes pattern from the feedback memory layer: premise-true + prescription-wrong = doubled effort. Caught before the doubled effort landed, thanks to tobi's explicit challenge + a session of walking through four options before picking one.
> 
> Closing.
